project_overview.project_development_objective: Increase utilization of quality, cost-effective, convergent, nutrition-specific and nutrition-sensitive services for pregnant and lactating women, adolescent girls and children under five years of age in select areas of the recipient's territory
project_overview.beneficiaries: Children under five years of age, pregnant and lactating women, adolescent girls, and their caregivers in 21 participating states. Direct beneficiaries estimated at 1.5 million people receiving basic nutrition services. Indirect beneficiaries include husbands, fathers, mothers, grandmothers, traditional and religious leaders, agricultural and community health extension workers, and public servants responsible for policy and service delivery

geographic_scope.fragile_or_conflict_affected_areas: yes - partial. Project explicitly targets fragile and conflict-affected areas. Tier 1 states include all states partially or fully in IPC 3+ (crisis to emergency food insecurity). The document notes armed insurgency in North-East, banditry in North-West, and farmer-pastoralist conflicts in Middle-Belt and Savanna regions. Project is marked as Fragile State(s) in financing modalities. Security considerations are explicitly addressed with reference to political, ethnic, or civil unrest risks in some areas

key_components.component_name: Component 1: Basic Package of Nutrition Services (BPNS)
key_components.description: Delivery of essential nutrition services at scale through primary healthcare system to protect vulnerable populations. Includes preventive and curative child nutrition interventions: micronutrient powders, deworming, zinc and ORS for diarrhea treatment, small-quantity lipid-based nutrient supplements (SQ-LNS), ready-to-use therapeutic foods (RUTF) for severe acute malnutrition treatment, vitamin A supplementation, multiple micronutrient supplementation for pregnant women, malaria prevention in pregnancy, and maternal infant and young child nutrition (MIYCN) counseling. Delivered through community health workers and health facilities. Includes social and behavior change communication and service delivery support/training.
key_components.financing: US$48.85 million (Sub-component 1.1: US$40.00 million; Sub-component 1.2: US$1.00 million; Sub-component 1.3: US$7.85 million)

key_components.description: Scale-up of homestead and community gardens to improve household access to nutritious, diverse, micronutrient-rich foods. Targets 1,000-day households, female-headed households, and those with adolescent mothers. Includes establishment of mixed demonstration farms and homestead gardens with climate-resilient crops (vegetables, legumes, tubers, biofortified crops, neglected tropical crops), small livestock (goats, poultry), agricultural input kits, micro-irrigation equipment, rainwater harvesting systems, training on climate-smart agricultural practices, food processing and preservation, water management, soil conservation, and pest management. Includes service delivery costs for training agricultural extension workers and supportive supervision.
key_components.financing: US$24.80 million (Sub-component 2.1: US$17.20 million; Sub-component 2.2: US$7.60 million)

key_components.component_name: Component 3: Data-Driven Multisectoral Coordination and Accountability
key_components.description: Strengthens coordination and accountability functions at national and state levels. Includes: (3.1) Nutrition-responsive public financial management with budget tagging and tracking system expansion to all 36 states to monitor nutrition and climate-related allocations and expenditures; (3.2) Annual State of Nutrition Report providing comprehensive assessment of nutrition and food security landscape with public disclosure; (3.3) Intervention mapping for multisectoral coordination to map nutrition actors and interventions and enhance coordination through State Committees on Food and Nutrition; (3.4) Project management and delivery including M&E, operational costs of project management units, and tracking of convergence.
key_components.financing: US$6.35 million (Sub-component 3.1: US$0.50 million; Sub-component 3.2: US$0.35 million; Sub-component 3.3: US$0.50 million; Sub-component 3.4: US$5.00 million)

implementation_arrangements.implementing_agency: Federal Ministry of Health and Social Welfare (FMOHSW) leads overall project management. Multi-sectoral coordination led by Federal Ministry of Budget and Economic Planning (FMBEP). Component 1 led by FMOHSW/Department of Nutrition. Component 2 led by Federal Ministry of Agriculture and Food Security (FMAFS). Component 3 led by FMBEP/Department of Social Development. National Project Management Unit (NPMU) at federal level coordinates day-to-day administration. State Project Management Units (SPMUs) in 21 participating states provide state-level oversight. State Ministries of Health, Agriculture, and Budget and Economic Planning implement components at state level through State Primary Health Care Development Agencies (SPHCDAs) and State Committees on Food and Nutrition
implementation_arrangements.coordination_mechanisms: Multi-sectoral coordination through National Nutrition Council (FMBEP and Office of Vice President) and State Committees on Food and Nutrition. Federal-state coordination through subsidiary agreements. Coordination with National Primary Healthcare Development Agency (NPHCDA) for health component oversight. Coordination with development partners through intervention mapping system. Ward Development Committees ensure reach to vulnerable populations. Partnership with civil society through Partnership for Amplified Voice platform. Coordination with National Social Register for targeting. Coordination with Nigerian Police for security of goods transport
implementation_arrangements.procurement_approach: World Bank Procurement Regulations for IPF Borrowers (February 2025) applied. Coordinated procurement approach for all nutrition services at federal level. Simplified Project Procurement Strategy for Development (PPSD) and 18-month procurement plan prepared. Key procurement activities include RUTF, preventive nutritional supplements/commodities, agricultural input kits, and consultancy services. Systematic Tracking of Exchanges in Procurement (STEP) used to plan, record, and track transactions. Retroactive financing up to US$32 million proposed for eligible expenditures. Framework agreements for recurring needs. UN agencies engaged for supplies where necessary. Hands-on Expanded Implementation Support (HEIS) for procurement assistance. Experienced procurement specialists to be recruited at NPMU and SPMUs
implementation_arrangements.monitoring_and_evaluation: Robust M&E framework with monitoring at federal, state, health facility, and community levels. NPMU and SPMUs responsible for overall monitoring and reporting to World Bank. M&E specialists in NPMU and SPMUs coordinate data collection with implementing ministries. Quarterly progress reports to World Bank (within 2 weeks of quarter end). Annual reports (4 weeks after year end). Data sources include District Health Management Information System 2 (DHIS-2), Community Health Management Information System (CHMIS), National Demographic and Health Survey (NDHS) 2023 for baselines, mini-DHS in 2026 for PDO indicators, National Social Register, and project management information systems. PDO indicators tracked: children 6-23 months receiving micronutrient powders, children 6-59 months receiving zinc/ORS for diarrhea, children under 5 treated for SAM, pregnant women with minimum dietary diversity, children 6-23 months with minimum dietary diversity. Intermediate indicators track BPNS coverage, SQ-LNS coverage, people fed from agricultural production, climate-resilient gardens established, states with intervention mapping, states with nutrition-responsive budgets, LGAs with quarterly coordination meetings, and grievances addressed within timeline

identified_risks.risk_description: Political instability, weak institutional capacity, and coordination challenges across three tiers of government (federal, state, local). Fragmented response to food and nutrition security crisis. Institutional framework complications with intersectoral and bidirectional reporting lines. Weak capacity in some states for implementation
identified_risks.mitigation_measures: Lessons from ANRiN Project on joint planning, synergized targeting, and joint reporting. Intervention mapping system to boost visibility and transparency of stakeholder investments. Subsidiary agreements between federal and participating states. Strong federal oversight through NPMU. Performance management system and performance frameworks. State Committees on Food and Nutrition to hold implementing line ministries accountable. Regular briefings to State Executive Councils on implementation progress

identified_risks.risk_description: High inflation (32%+ in August 2024), food inflation (37%+ in August 2024), currency volatility, weak purchasing power of households, economic distress affecting food and nutrition security. Average household income (NGN 1,255/day or US$0.78) insufficient to afford nutritious diet against minimum wage of NGN 70,000 (US$43.8)
identified_risks.mitigation_measures: Project designed to rapidly scale up nutrition interventions and food production to address immediate crisis. Homestead gardens to improve household food security and reduce reliance on markets. Targeting of poorest and most vulnerable households using National Social Register. Convergence of nutrition-specific and nutrition-sensitive interventions to maximize impact with available resources

identified_risks.risk_description: Weak sectoral policies and strategies, low domestic nutrition spending (less than 0.2% of health expenditure in 2019), fragmented nutrition investments, limited coordination among sectors and development partners
identified_risks.mitigation_measures: Alignment with Nigeria's National Development Plan 2021-2025, Nigeria Agenda 2050, National Multisectoral Plan of Action for Food and Nutrition, and Nutrition-774 local action initiative. Budget tagging and tracking system to improve resource mobilization and align with nutrition policy priorities. State of Nutrition Report for evidence-based decision-making. Intervention mapping to coordinate investments and minimize duplication

identified_risks.risk_category: Technical Design of Project or Program
identified_risks.risk_description: Complexity of multi-sectoral convergence approach, need to integrate nutrition services across health and agriculture sectors, technical capacity gaps in service delivery, potential for ineffective implementation of complex interventions
identified_risks.mitigation_measures: Building on proven ANRiN Project experience and global evidence on cost-effective nutrition interventions. Geospatial Optima Nutrition analyses to optimize resource allocation. Use of digital tools (ANRiN app integrated into DHIS-2) for efficiency and transparency. Training and capacity building for health workers and agricultural extension workers. Supportive supervision and mentoring. Integration of lessons learned from ANRiN Project. Simplified and context-specific approaches engaging local stakeholders

identified_risks.risk_category: Institutional Capacity for Implementation and Sustainability
identified_risks.risk_description: Weak institutional capacity at federal and state levels, limited experience with large-scale implementation, capacity gaps in project management, financial management, procurement, and monitoring. Federated structure of Nigeria complicates coordination. Sustainability concerns for community health worker programs and agricultural extension services
identified_risks.mitigation_measures: Hands-on Expanded Implementation Support (HEIS) for implementation assistance. Recruitment of experienced project management and technical staff at NPMU and SPMUs. Training and capacity building for project staff in financial management, procurement, and M&E. Leveraging existing institutional structures (SPHCDAs, State Committees on Food and Nutrition). Integration with ongoing government programs (NHSRII, Primary Healthcare Provision Strengthening Program, Nigeria Human Capital Opportunities for Prosperity and Equity–Governance program). Decentralized implementation leveraging state leadership. Risk-based internal audit methodology. Continuous capacity development

identified_risks.risk_description: Substantial fiduciary risk due to federated structure of Nigeria with multiple implementing entities at federal and state levels, weak internal audit function, potential for funds not being used for intended purposes, weak capacity in procurement contract management and technical specifications preparation, governance issues including integrity and non-compliance, security challenges impacting contractor/supplier performance
identified_risks.mitigation_measures: Robust financial management arrangements through existing Project Financial Management Units (PFMUs) at state level and Federal Project Financial Management Department (FPFMD) at federal level. Accounting on International Public Sector Accounting Standards (IPSAS) accrual basis. Robust Chart of Accounts for tracking expenditures by categories, components, and activities. Monthly returns to Accountant General. Interim Financial Reports (IFRs) prepared semi-annually and submitted within 45 days. Independent internal audit with Risk Based Internal Audit (RBIA) methodology. Quarterly consolidated internal audit reports. External audit by independent auditor with consolidated annual financial statements audited within 6 months of year end. Adequate internal controls with segregation of duties. Training and capacity building for financial management staff. Enhanced accountability framework for soft expenditures. Regular reporting and supervision. Designated Accounts at Central Bank of Nigeria. Advance-based funding with quarterly reporting requirements before subsequent advances. Retroactive financing up to US$32 million for eligible expenditures. Recruitment of experienced procurement specialists. Strengthened contract management structures. Aggregated federal-level procurement for major items. Framework agreements for recurring needs. Engagement of UN agencies for supplies where necessary

identified_risks.risk_description: Moderate environmental and social risks. Potential environmental impacts from distribution of health commodities (packaging waste, expired commodities), homestead gardening and farming (animal waste disposal, odor nuisances), transportation safety risks. Social risks include exclusion of vulnerable groups and ethnic minorities, transmission of communicable diseases, community exposure to medical waste, gender-based violence (GBV), sexual exploitation and abuse (SEA), sexual harassment (SH), elite capture, inadequate stakeholder engagement, grievances, intimate partner violence. Security risks in conflict-affected areas. Displacement or refugee concerns in conflict zones
identified_risks.mitigation_measures: Environmental and Social Code of Practice (ESCoP) to be developed at national level with site-specific measures for identified risks. Updated Health Care Waste Management Plan to manage waste from health facilities. Standard Operating Procedures (SOPs) for security and safety risk management adapted from ANRiN Project. Occupational Health and Safety (OHS) measures consistent with National Policy on Occupational Health and Safety. Resource efficiency and pollution prevention measures in ESCoP. Road safety risk assessment and management. Abridged Code of Conduct for Sexual Exploitation and Abuse (SEA) and Sexual Harassment (SH). Security Management Plan addressing political, ethnic, or civil unrest. Gender and GBV Action Plan across states. Grievance Redress Mechanism (GRM) updated and accessible to all project-affected parties. Stakeholder Engagement Plan (SEP) for continuous engagement. Social accountability mechanisms to prevent elite capture. Differentiated approaches for vulnerable groups including Persons with Disabilities, women in remote areas, and ethnic minorities. Targeting through National Social Register. Ward Development Committees to ensure reach to marginalized populations. Partnership with civil society through Partnership for Amplified Voice. Community mobilization and engagement officers. Training of CHWs on climate change, health, and protection measures. Partnership with Nigerian Police for security of goods transport. Community awareness and sensitization on climate and health impacts

identified_risks.risk_description: Risk of inadequate stakeholder engagement, community resistance, elite capture, lack of community ownership, potential for grievances and disputes, limited engagement of civil society organizations, weak community participation in planning and monitoring
identified_risks.mitigation_measures: Stakeholder Engagement Plan (SEP) for continuous stakeholder engagement. Project-specific Grievance Redress Mechanism (GRM) updated and accessible at various levels, culturally appropriate, at no cost, without retribution, including anonymous complaints. GRM indicator to measure efficiency (grievances addressed within approved timeline). Civil society organization platform (Partnership for Amplified Voice) for transparent decision-making and monitoring. Ward Development Committees to ensure marginalized and vulnerable groups connected to services. Community mobilization and engagement officers. Faith-based partnerships for MIYCN messaging. Community health workers as trusted local actors. Localized solutions and engagement of local actors. Public disclosure of State of Nutrition Report to introduce healthy competition between states and inspire government action. Feedback mechanisms from community members on knowledge, attitudes, and practices

identified_risks.risk_description: Security risks in fragile and conflict-affected areas, particularly North-East (armed insurgency), North-West (banditry), and Middle-Belt/Savanna (farmer-pastoralist conflicts). Risk to beneficiaries and project staff including community workers. Risk of interception of goods and commodities by miscreant elements. Potential disruption of service delivery due to insecurity
identified_risks.mitigation_measures: Leveraging existing state-specific security management plans from health projects. Partnership with Nigerian Police to ensure goods and commodities transported without interception. Security Management Plan to be adopted within 60 days of Effective Date. Community-based delivery model using trusted local CHWs to foster relationships and build trust with local populations. Real-time information on climate shocks to help CHWs plan community outreach. Training of CHWs on protection measures. Localized solutions and engagement of local actors. Flexibility in implementation approaches for different contexts

identified_risks.risk_description: Nigeria ranks 152 out of 187 countries on climate vulnerability and readiness. High exposure to climate shocks including extreme heat, floods, droughts, sea-level rise, erosion, storms, wildfires. 24% of population (41 million people) live in high climate exposure areas. Climate change threatens agricultural productivity (only 1% of agriculture irrigated), food security, and nutrition outcomes. Projected temperature increase of 2.9-5.7°C by end of century. Heat waves projected to increase 8-55 additional days. Extreme rainfall, floods, and droughts projected to increase in severity and intensity. Climate change combined with conflict and violence has led 19 million Nigerians to face food insecurity crisis. One-unit increase in temperature increases probability of child stunting by 0.53-0.71% per year. Malaria and dengue prevalence expected to increase. Heat-related illnesses including meningitis projected to increase. Women and children disproportionately affected
identified_risks.mitigation_measures: Project activities designed to address climate change as primary driver of malnutrition crisis. Component 1 (BPNS) targets climate-vulnerable populations, particularly in first 1,000 days. CHWs equipped with real-time climate shock information to plan community outreach. Training of CHWs on climate change, health links, and protection measures. Climate-resilient storage measures (flood-proof, temperature-regulated) for nutrition supplements and agricultural inputs. Component 2 focuses on climate-resilient homestead gardens with short-duration, high-yield crop varieties adaptable to diverse ecological conditions. Rainwater harvesting systems and climate-resilient irrigation methods (drip, spray, solar water pumping). Soil conservation practices (mulching, weeding, cover-cropping, crop rotation). Small livestock selection (goats, poultry) more resistant and adaptable to climate shocks. Updated homestead gardening manuals with climate-smart approaches. Component 3 includes climate budget tagging to monitor climate-related expenditures. State of Nutrition Report includes sections on climate change impacts. Intervention mapping includes climate-related nutrition interventions. Training of SCFN officials on climate and nutrition data. Early warning/early action programs to prevent malnutrition in extreme weather events. Project aligned with Paris Agreement, Nigeria's NDCs (20% GHG reduction target, conditional 47% by 2030), National Adaptation Strategy and Plan of Action on Climate Change, National Adaptation Plan Framework, and National Climate Change Policy 2021-2030. All project activities considered Universally Aligned and not anticipated to contribute to GHG emissions

triggered_safeguard_policies: ESS 1: Assessment and Management of Environmental and Social Risks and Impacts (Relevant)
ESS 10: Stakeholder Engagement and Information Disclosure (Relevant)
ESS 3: Resource Efficiency and Pollution Prevention and Management (Relevant)
safeguards_and_social.grievance_redress_mechanism: yes - Project-specific Grievance Redress Mechanism (GRM) developed under ANRiN Project and updated for ANRiN 2.0. GRM Manual updated within 30 days of Effective Date to be consistent with relevant ESSs. Mechanism provides information, facilitates communication, resolves disputes promptly, builds trust, and creates platform for communities to express views on project activities. Accessible to all project-affected parties at no cost and without retribution, including anonymous complaints. Culturally appropriate and readily accessible. Grievances addressed within project-approved timeline (indicator targets 45% in Period 1 and 90% at closing). World Bank Grievance Redress Service (GRS) also available for project-affected communities and individuals. World Bank Accountability Mechanism (Inspection Panel and Dispute Resolution Service) available for complaints regarding World Bank non-compliance with policies
safeguards_and_social.gender_considerations: Project explicitly addresses gender and vulnerability. Women and girls are primary beneficiaries with focus on pregnant and lactating women and adolescent girls. Project recognizes gender nutrition gap - approximately 58% of pregnant women in Nigeria are anemic. Early marriage, premature childbearing, and high fertility rates (6.1-5.9 children per woman in North-East and North-West) contribute to women's limited influence on household food and dietary choices. Children of adolescent mothers 33% more likely to be stunted. Pregnant women with disabilities face neglect. Project targets 1,000-day households, female-headed households (3.6% of NSR), and those with adolescent mothers (24.2% of NSR). Homestead gardens targeted to female-headed households and those with adolescent mothers. Community gardens prioritized for women owner and cultivator clusters. Project aims to close gender gap in agriculture - women contribute 40-60% of agricultural labor and manage 60-90% of processing tasks but own few land and receive less than 10% of available credit. Equal access to resources could boost yields by 20-30%. Project expected to increase women's control over agricultural production and intra-household decision-making through economic independence. Gender and GBV Action Plan to be developed and implemented across states. SBC communication promotes shared childcare responsibilities and women's agency in household nutrition decisions including for adolescent mothers. Female community outreach coordinators to reach rural women. Differentiated approaches to prevent exclusion of women in remote areas. GBV can negatively impact women and adolescent girls health and nutrition outcomes - survivors experience disrupted eating habits and lower exclusive breastfeeding rates. Project mitigates GBV risks through social accountability mechanisms, SEP, efficient GRM, and Gender/GBV Action Plan. Abridged Code of Conduct for SEA/SH to be adopted within 60 days of Effective Date
vulnerable_groups: Children under five years of age (primary focus on first 1,000 days from conception to age 2)
Persons with Disabilities (to be targeted through NSR)
Internally displaced persons and refugees (in conflict-affected areas)
Populations in North-East, North-West, and Middle-Belt regions with highest malnutrition and food insecurity burden

fcv_relevant_details.conflict_sensitivity: Project explicitly designed with conflict sensitivity. Recognizes that armed insurgency in North-East, banditry in North-West, and farmer-pastoralist conflicts in Middle-Belt and Savanna have adversely affected food and nutrition security. Project targets fragile and conflict-affected areas (marked as Fragile State(s) in financing modalities). Uses community-based delivery model through trusted local CHWs to foster relationships and build trust with local populations in contexts with deep-seated mistrust between community and formal health care system. Leverages existing state-specific security management plans from health projects. Partnership with Nigerian Police for security of goods transport. Localized solutions and engagement of local actors recognized as more effective than one-size-fits-all approach. Project designed to prevent further deterioration in nutritional outcomes and social and economic instability. Addresses fragility and food insecurity as CPF objective. Recognizes that conflict combined with climate shocks and economic distress creates unprecedented food and nutrition security crisis. Project aims to prevent negative coping strategies and humanitarian emergencies
fcv_relevant_details.fragility_context: Nigeria faces multiple fragility drivers: (1) Armed conflict and violence - armed insurgency in North-East, banditry in North-West, farmer-pastoralist conflicts in Middle-Belt and Savanna region; (2) Food and nutrition security crisis of unprecedented scale - 32 million people (15.9% of analyzed population) faced crisis to emergency levels of food insecurity (IPC Phase 3-4) during June-October 2024 lean season. One in three children stunted (39.5% nationally, up from 37% baseline). Wasting increasing to 8% from 6.8%. Estimated 10 million acutely malnourished children in 2024, with 2.6 million suffering from severe acute malnutrition. One in three children experiences severe child food poverty due to conflict, climate crises, and inequity; (3) Economic distress - annual inflation surpassed 32% in August 2024, food inflation 37%+, staple food costs increased 185%+. Average household income (NGN 1,255/day or US$0.78) insufficient to afford nutritious diet. At least one-third of households cannot afford lowest-cost nutritious diet; (4) Climate change - Nigeria ranks 152 out of 187 on climate vulnerability and readiness. 24% of population (41 million people) live in high climate exposure areas. Extreme heat, floods, droughts, sea-level rise, erosion, storms, wildfires projected to increase in frequency and severity. Climate change combined with conflict has led 19 million Nigerians to face food insecurity crisis; (5) Weak institutional capacity and governance - fragmented response to crisis, weak coordination among sectors and development partners, low domestic nutrition spending (less than 0.2% of health expenditure in 2019), limited sectoral policies and strategies; (6) Geographic disparities - stunting most prevalent in North-West (53%) and lowest in South-South (18%). Wasting approximately twice as high in North-East (17%) and three times as high in North-West (34%) as in other regions. Rural stunting (48%) almost twice as high as urban (28%). Disparities across socioeconomic groups with stunting high even in highest income quintile (15%)
fcv_relevant_details.security_considerations: Project acknowledges security risks in fragile and conflict-affected areas. Specific security challenges identified: (1) Political, ethnic, or civil unrest in some areas poses risk to beneficiaries and ANRiN 2.0 staff including community workers; (2) Potential for interception of goods and commodities by miscreant elements; (3) Potential disruption of service delivery due to insecurity; (4) Deep-seated mistrust between community and formal health care system in some areas. Mitigation measures: (1) Leveraging existing state-specific security management plans from health projects; (2) Partnership with Nigerian Police to ensure goods and commodities transported without interception; (3) Security Management Plan to be adopted within 60 days of Effective Date and implemented throughout project; (4) Community-based delivery model using trusted local CHWs to foster relationships and build trust; (5) Real-time information on climate shocks to help CHWs plan community outreach on safe days; (6) Training of CHWs on protection measures; (7) Localized solutions and engagement of local actors; (8) Flexibility in implementation approaches for different contexts. Project recognizes that CHWs can play pivotal role in fostering relationships and building trust with local populations in labile contexts
fcv_relevant_details.displacement_or_refugees: yes - Project operates in conflict-affected areas with potential for displacement and refugee populations. Document references internally displaced persons and refugees in context of conflict-affected areas (North-East, North-West, Middle-Belt). Project targets vulnerable populations including those affected by conflict and violence. Homestead gardens and nutrition services designed to support food security and nutrition of displaced and vulnerable populations. Project uses National Social Register for targeting, which can capture displaced populations. Ward Development Committees tasked with ensuring marginalized and vulnerable groups connected to services. However, specific displacement or refugee-focused interventions not explicitly detailed in PAD. Project does not appear to have specific resettlement or displacement management provisions, though Environmental and Social Framework addresses potential risks. Project designed to prevent further deterioration and humanitarian emergencies that could result from food and nutrition crisis in conflict-affected areas
